title: National Workshop on Public Lending Right: Strengthening Moldova’s Path Toward EU Integration
content:
Blog post by Mariana Harjevschi

On 23 and 24  April 2026, coinciding with World Book and Copyright Day, the National Workshop on Public Lending Right (PLR) took place in Chișinău in a hybrid format. The event brought together policymakers, international experts, and representatives of libraries, authors’ organisations, and cultural institutions to advance discussions on introducing Public Lending Right in the Republic of Moldova.

The workshop was organised by the State Agency on Intellectual Property (AGEPI) with the support of the EU4IP project and Swiss Partnership Moldova. It formed part of the broader national effort to align copyright legislation with the EU acquis and to explore sustainable policy options for implementing PLR.

Strengthening Dialogue Between Policy and Practice

title: 10 Takeaways From the Twin EU Consultations About the Landscape for Copyright Reform
content:
In the past weeks, we have seen the launch of two consultations relating to the European Union’s legislation about copyright, and how it affects the ability of researchers, educators, learners and the libraries that support them to work.

The results of these will, a priori, play an important role in shaping the direction of travel of the European Union’s future work in this area, and in particular, potential legislation next year (tentatively titled ‘Directive enhancing the copyright environment for European creativity and innovation’).

The first consultation is an open Call for Evidence (25 June deadline) on what is needed in order to create ‘a better European copyright environment for

title: Cyprus Event Report: Copyright and Access in the Age of AI
content:
Blog post by Ypapanti (Dia) Kytta | CALIS

Greek version available here

On 11 May 2026, Knowledge Rights 21, the Foundation for Parliamentarism and Participatory Democracy of the House of Representatives of the Republic of Cyprus, and the Cyprus Association of Librarians and Information Scientists (CALIS) co-organised an event in Nicosia entitled “Copyright and Access in the Age of Artificial Intelligence.” The event focused on the challenges posed by the rapid development of AI for knowledge, cultural heritage, and rights of access to information, with particular emphasis on the Cypriot context.

The event brought together representatives from libraries, universities, research and cultural institutions, members of governmental committees on AI and the European Union, and legal experts specialising in intellectual property law.

title: Cyprus Copyright Law, Research, and Education: A System Rich in Ambition, but Strained by Its Own Architecture
content:
Blog post by Professor Philippe Jougleux

Explore the Report

Copyright law rarely attracts public attention unless a dispute reaches the courts or a controversy emerges around technology. Yet, for universities, libraries, researchers, teachers, and students, copyright rules shape everyday academic life. They determine what may be shared in a classroom, what can be digitised by a library, how research outputs circulate, and whether innovation is facilitated or delayed.

However, in this aspect, Cyprus is trying to do several demanding things at once. It wants to support research, teaching, libraries, cultural heritage, and op

title: World IP Day 2026: Uneven Performance or Team Europe?
content:
Download KR21’s infographic

A key justification for intellectual property rules is their contribution to innovation. The prevailing logic suggests that the creation of (time-limited) monopolies allows creators to recoup the costs of developing new ideas.

This argument is relatively straightforward for measures such as patents, trademarks, or design rights (although concerns about abuses persist). 

However, it is more complicated regarding copyright. This doesn’t just regulate the creative industries, but equally affects citizens, scientists and researchers. The research process is explicitly about accessing and building on existing facts and insights. As such, anything that causes barriers to, or exclusion from, this needs to be strictly evaluated. Copyright needs to be designed with this objective in mind. 

Picking up (tangentially) on the theme of this year’s

title: Every Time a Library Cannot Do Its Job, the Public Interest Loses: Tell Us Your Story
content:
The mission of libraries is under threat. Not from budget cuts or censorship alone, but from a quieter crisis: a legal and market framework that is systematically limiting libraries’ ability to function. Publishers can refuse to license digital materials. Contracts can override rights granted by law. Digital locks can make legal activities impossible. Copyright rules written for classrooms leave libraries out. The result is the same every time: a user who needed something, and a library that could not provide it.

Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) named these barriers in its action plan, Safeguard Access, Empower Europe: An Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries, launched in February 2026. Today, 23 April, on UNESCO’s World Book and Copyright Day, we are taking the next step.

Launching the #LetLibrariesBeLibraries Case Study Repository

title: Moldova Symposium Report: Advocating to Let Libraries Be Libraries
content:
Blog post by Mariana Harjevschi

On 25 March 2026, Mariana Harjevschi, KR21 national coordinator for Moldova, presented the KR21 roadmap “Safeguard Access, Empower Europe: An Action Plan to Let Libraries be Libraries” at the 35th National Symposium “The Library Year 2025”, held in Chișinău. See the recording of Mariana’s intervention.

title: Different Players, Same Game: What Libraries Have in Common With Gamers
content:
The European Citizens’ Initiative (ECI) on the right to resell digital games is on the agenda of a joint hearing by the European Parliament’s Internal Market and Consumer Protection (IMCO), Legal Affairs (JURI), and Petitions (PETI) Committee on 16 April.

The ECI addresses a practice that many consumers know well: buying a game on the understanding that they own it, only to find it switched off or deleted by the company that sold it. Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) supports the push for action, and we have a direct interest in its outcome, because the problem the ECI identifies is not specific to games.

Libraries Face the Same Structural Imbalance

Libraries and the researchers, educators, learners, and citizens who depend on them operate under conditions that are structurally identical to those the ECI describes. Licensed digital collections disappear without warning. Acc

title: Copyright Maximalism Will Stifle a Research-Intensive Digital Economy
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

Originally published on 26 March 2026 on the Times Higher Education

On 18 March, after more than two years of deliberating what to do about artificial intelligence and copyright, the UK government has announced the need to “gather more evidence on how copyright laws are impacting the development and deployment of AI across the economy and the economic benefits of reform”.

Perhaps the reason for this delay has been minsters’ growing realisation that the issues concerning AI and copyright are far more complicated than the “Big Music v Big Tech” paradigm bein
